1	# Plugin format (our canonical, self-contained reference)
2	
3	This is the authoritative description of every file format `agent-architect` generates. It is
4	deliberately self-contained: generation must never depend on any external plugin being installed,
5	on a docs site being reachable, or on a version-brittle cache path. If a format detail matters for
6	generation, it is captured here.
7	
8	## Table of contents
9	1. Plugin directory layout
10	2. `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`
11	3. `skills/<name>/SKILL.md`
12	4. `agents/<name>.md`
13	5. `commands/<name>.md`
14	6. `.mcp.json`
15	7. `marketplace.json` (listing)
16	8. Conventions & gotchas
17	
18	---
19	
20	## 1. Plugin directory layout
21	
22	A plugin is a directory whose `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` declares it. Everything else is
23	optional and discovered by convention:
24	
25	```
26	<plugin-root>/
27	├── .claude-plugin/
28	│   └── plugin.json            # REQUIRED — the manifest
29	├── commands/                  # optional — one .md per slash command
30	│   └── <command>.md
31	├── skills/                    # optional — one subdir per skill
32	│   └── <skill>/
33	│       ├── SKILL.md           # REQUIRED for the skill
34	│       ├── references/        # docs the skill reads on demand
35	│       ├── scripts/           # executable helpers (run as python -m scripts.<name>)
36	│       └── assets/            # templates / brand files used in output
37	├── agents/                    # optional — one .md per subagent
38	│   └── <agent>.md
39	└── .mcp.json                  # optional — MCP server declarations
40	```
41	
42	Folder name, plugin `name`, and the primary command name should share one kebab-case identifier.
43	
44	---
45	
46	## 2. `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`
47	
48	The manifest. JSON (no comments).
49	
50	| Field | Req? | Notes |
51	|---|---|---|
52	| `name` | yes | kebab-case; matches the plugin folder. |
53	| `description` | yes | One line, "what + when to use". Drives discovery — make it specific and a little pushy so it triggers. |
54	| `author` | yes | Object `{ "name": "...", "email": "..." }`. |
55	| `version` | no | Semver string, e.g. `"0.1.0"`. Start at `0.1.0`. |
56	| `license` | no | SPDX id or `"UNLICENSED"`. |
57	| `category` | no | e.g. `"developer-tools"`, `"strategy"`, `"research"`. Aids marketplace placement. |
58	| `keywords` | no | Array of strings for searchability. |
59	
60	Example:
61	
62	```json
63	{
64	  "name": "standup-bot",
65	  "description": "Drafts your daily standup from git activity and your task tracker. Use when the user asks to write a standup, summarize what they did yesterday, or prep for daily sync.",
66	  "version": "0.1.0",
67	  "author": { "name": "Lucas Zhu", "email": "you@example.com" },
68	  "license": "UNLICENSED",
69	  "category": "developer-tools",
70	  "keywords": ["standup", "git", "productivity"]
71	}
72	```
73	
74	---
75	
76	## 3. `skills/<name>/SKILL.md`
77	
78	Markdown with YAML frontmatter, then a markdown body of instructions.
79	
80	**Allowed frontmatter keys** (a validator enforces this set — do not invent others):
81	
82	| Key | Req? | Notes |
83	|---|---|---|
84	| `name` | yes | Skill identifier (kebab-case). |
85	| `description` | yes | The primary triggering mechanism. Include BOTH what it does AND when to use it. Make it pushy — skills tend to under-trigger. All "when to use" info lives here, not in the body. |
86	| `license` | no | SPDX id or `"UNLICENSED"`. |
87	| `allowed-tools` | no | Restrict the tools the skill may use (comma- or list-form). |
88	| `metadata` | no | Free-form object for build bookkeeping. |
89	| `compatibility` | no | Required tools / environment notes (rarely needed). |
90	
91	Body guidance (progressive disclosure):
92	- Keep SKILL.md lean (ideally < 500 lines). Push detail into `references/` and point to it with
93	  clear "read this when…" guidance.
94	- Explain the *why* behind instructions rather than piling on rigid MUSTs — the model has good
95	  theory of mind and works better when it understands intent.
96	- Put deterministic / repetitive work in `scripts/` and call them, instead of describing the steps
97	  in prose. Scripts run as `python -m scripts.<name>` with cwd = the skill dir.
98	
99	```markdown
100	---
101	name: standup-bot
102	description: Drafts a daily standup from git activity and the user's task tracker. Use whenever the user asks to write/prep a standup, summarize yesterday's work, or get ready for daily sync.
103	---
104	
105	# Standup Bot
106	... instructions ...
107	```
108	
109	---
110	
111	## 4. `agents/<name>.md` (subagents)
112	
113	A subagent is an isolated worker the main session spawns for a heavy or context-polluting step.
114	Markdown with YAML frontmatter; the body is the agent's system prompt.
115	
116	| Key | Req? | Notes |
117	|---|---|---|
118	| `name` | yes | Identifier the spawning skill references. Must match exactly what skill bodies call. |
119	| `description` | yes | When to use this subagent. |
120	| `tools` | yes | The tools the agent may use. Comma-separated list (e.g. `Read, Grep, Bash`). Keep it minimal — least privilege. |
121	| `model` | no | `inherit` (default — use the caller's model), or a specific model id / alias. |
122	| `color` | no | UI hint. |
123	
124	```markdown
125	---
126	name: pr-reviewer
127	description: Reviews a single pull request diff for correctness and style. Spawn one per PR.
128	tools: Read, Grep, Bash
129	model: inherit
130	---
131	
132	You are a focused PR reviewer. ...
133	```
134	
135	---
136	
137	## 5. `commands/<name>.md` (slash commands)
138	
139	An explicit entry point the user invokes as `/<name>`. Markdown with YAML frontmatter; the body is
140	the instruction executed when the command runs (it typically delegates to a skill).
141	
142	| Key | Req? | Notes |
143	|---|---|---|
144	| `description` | yes | What the command does (shown in the command list). |
145	| `argument-hint` | no | Placeholder shown after the command name, e.g. `"[pr number]"`. |
146	| `allowed-tools` | no | Restrict tools available while the command runs. |
147	| `disable-model-invocation` | no | Boolean; if true the command is only user-invokable, not model-invokable. |
148	
149	```markdown
150	---
151	description: Review a pull request for correctness and style.
152	argument-hint: "[pr number or url]"
153	---
154	
155	Invoke the pr-review skill on $ARGUMENTS.
156	```
157	
158	---
159	
160	## 6. `.mcp.json`
161	
162	Declares MCP servers the plugin needs. JSON object whose **keys are server names** and whose values
163	describe the transport. The user completes any auth — never commit secrets.
164	
165	Two value shapes:
166	
167	**Command-based (stdio)** — a local process:
168	
169	```json
170	{
171	  "mcpServers": {
172	    "context7": {
173	      "command": "npx",
174	      "args": ["-y", "@upstash/context7-mcp"]
175	    }
176	  }
177	}
178	```
179	
180	**HTTP-based** — a remote endpoint:
181	
182	```json
183	{
184	  "mcpServers": {
185	    "github": {
186	      "type": "http",
187	      "url": "https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/",
188	      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer ${GITHUB_TOKEN}" }
189	    }
190	  }
191	}
192	```
193	
194	Notes:
195	- Some hosts expect the servers under a top-level `mcpServers` object (shown above); others accept
196	  server names at the top level. Generate the `mcpServers`-wrapped form — it is the most widely
197	  accepted.
198	- For command transport: `command` + `args` (+ optional `env`).
199	- For http transport: `type: "http"`, `url`, optional `headers` (auth via `${ENV_VAR}` placeholders
200	  the user fills, not literal secrets).
201	
202	---
203	
204	## 7. `marketplace.json` (listing)
205	
206	Only needed for a `client-ready` deliverable that will be distributed through a marketplace. It
207	lists one or more plugins available from a repository.
208	
209	```json
210	{
211	  "name": "lucas-consulting-agents",
212	  "owner": { "name": "Lucas Zhu", "email": "you@example.com" },
213	  "plugins": [
214	    {
215	      "name": "standup-bot",
216	      "source": "./standup-bot",
217	      "description": "Drafts your daily standup from git activity and your task tracker.",
218	      "version": "0.1.0",
219	      "category": "developer-tools",
220	      "keywords": ["standup", "git", "productivity"]
221	    }
222	  ]
223	}
224	```
225	
226	- `source` is a path (or git ref) to the plugin directory relative to the marketplace file.
227	- The per-plugin fields mirror that plugin's `plugin.json` — keep them in sync (the assembly pass
228	  derives them from the spec, not by re-reading the manifest).
229	
230	---
231	
232	## 8. Conventions & gotchas
233	
234	- **One kebab-case name** shared by folder / plugin / primary command.
235	- **Least-privilege tools** on every agent and (where it matters) skill.
236	- **Descriptions are load-bearing**: skill and plugin descriptions are how Claude decides to use
237	  them. Specific + pushy > vague.
238	- **No secrets in `.mcp.json`** — use `${ENV_VAR}` placeholders; auth is the user's job.
239	- **Windows reality**: scripts are invoked `python -m scripts.<name>` with cwd = the owning skill
240	  dir; paths can contain spaces — always use absolute paths and quote them.
241	- **Agent name drift**: a skill body that says "spawn the `pr-reviewer` agent" must match the
242	  agent file's frontmatter `name`. The assembly pass reconciles this.
243

1	# Component recipes — deterministic per-type generation guidance
2	
3	This is the recipe the `plugin-generator` subagent follows so that independently, parallel-generated
4	components come out **consistent and deterministic**. Each generator gets one component slice plus
5	the spec's `shared` block (see `architecture-spec.md` §8) and renders it into files using the rules
6	here. `plugin-format.md` is the authoritative file-format reference; this file maps **spec fields →
7	file content** and fixes conventions the format doc leaves open.
8	
9	Read this together with `plugin-format.md`. Where they overlap, `plugin-format.md` wins on *format*;
10	this file wins on *which spec field becomes what*.
11	
12	## Universal rules (all types)
13	
14	- **Output location**: write under `output_root` at the component's `rel_path`. Always use absolute
15	  paths; quote paths (Windows OneDrive paths contain spaces).
16	- **Source of truth**: the spec slice. Do not redesign; render faithfully. Never invent fields the
17	  spec didn't ask for.
18	- **Shared context**: reuse `shared.domain_context` verbatim where domain framing is needed, follow
19	  `shared.naming_convention`, and use `shared.global_glossary` terms consistently. This is the main
20	  anti-drift mechanism.
21	- **Descriptions are load-bearing**: for skill/agent/command, the `description` is how Claude decides
22	  to use the thing. Make it specific and a little pushy — state **what it does AND when to use it**.
23	- **Never write manifest/wiring files**: no `plugin.json`, `.mcp.json`, `marketplace.json`,
24	  `README.md`, `INSTALL.md`. Assembly owns those.
25	- **Receipt**: every generator returns the receipt in `architecture-spec.md` §9. The notes below say
26	  what goes in each receipt field per type.
27	
28	---
29	
30	## 1. `type: "skill"`
31	
32	### Files to create
33	- `<rel_path>/SKILL.md` — **required**.
34	- `<rel_path>/scripts/` + `scripts/__init__.py` + one `.py` per entry — **only if** `needs_scripts`
35	  is truthy (a bool `true` means "scripts likely needed"; a list names the entry points).
36	- `<rel_path>/references/` + one `.md` per topic — **only if** `needs_references` is a non-empty
37	  list (each entry is a doc topic).
38	- `<rel_path>/assets/` + placeholder template files — **only if** `needs_assets` is truthy.
39	
40	Do not create empty `scripts/`/`references/`/`assets/` dirs the spec didn't ask for.
41	
42	### Required frontmatter keys
43	`name`, `description`. Allowed (do not invent others): `name`, `description`, `license`,
44	`allowed-tools`, `metadata`, `compatibility`. Set `allowed-tools` only if the spec implies a
45	restriction; otherwise omit it.
46	
47	### Deriving the description (pushy, what + when)
48	Combine `description_seed` (the *what*) with `trigger_intent` (the *when*). Result: one or two
49	sentences — first the capability, then "Use when …" listing the trigger phrases. Skills under-trigger,
50	so be concrete and assertive. All "when to use" info lives in the description, not the body.
51	
52	Example mapping:
53	```
54	description_seed: "Drafts your daily standup from git activity and your Notion tasks, then tightens it."
55	trigger_intent:   "write my standup, summarize what I did yesterday, prep for daily sync"
56	=>
57	description: Drafts your daily standup from git activity and your Notion tasks, then tightens it for clarity. Use whenever the user wants to write or prep a standup, summarize what they did yesterday, or get ready for daily sync.
58	```
59	
60	### Field → content mapping
61	| Spec field | Becomes |
62	|---|---|
63	| `name` | frontmatter `name` (kebab-case, matches `rel_path` dir). |
64	| `description_seed` + `trigger_intent` | frontmatter `description` (see above). |
65	| `purpose` | the opening paragraph of the body (what the skill does / its value). |
66	| `needs_scripts` | `scripts/<name>.py` files + a "run `python -m scripts.<name>`" pointer in body. |
67	| `needs_references` | `references/<topic>.md` files + "read this when…" pointers in body. |
68	| `needs_assets` | `assets/` templates + a body note on when to use them. |
69	| `depends_on` | body references to those siblings **by name** (spawn the `<agent name>` agent; use the `<server_name>` MCP server). |
70	
71	### Body shape (lean — keep < ~500 lines, use progressive disclosure)
72	```markdown
73	---
74	name: standup
75	description: <pushy what + when, derived as above>
76	---
77	
78	# Standup
79	
80	<one paragraph from `purpose`, grounded in shared.domain_context>
81	
82	## How it works
83	- <high-level steps; defer detail to references>
84	- Run `python -m scripts.collect_git_activity` to gather commits.   # if needs_scripts
85	- Read `references/standup-format.md` for the output format.        # if needs_references
86	- Spawn the `standup-reviewer` agent to tighten the draft.          # if depends_on an agent
87	- Use the `notion` MCP server to read the task board.               # if depends_on an mcp
88	```
89	
90	### Receipt notes
91	- `final_description` = the description written.
92	- `declared_tools` = `allowed-tools` if set, else `[]`.
93	- `references_to` = ids from `depends_on` that the body actually references.
94	- `scripts_entrypoints` = `python -m scripts.<name>` for each script written.
95	
96	---
97	
98	## 2. `type: "agent"`
99	
100	### Files to create
101	- `<rel_path>` (e.g. `agents/<name>.md`) — a single markdown file. Nothing else.
102	
103	### Required frontmatter keys
104	`name`, `description`, `tools`. Optional: `model` (default `inherit`), `color`.
105	
106	### Field → content mapping
107	| Spec field | Becomes |
108	|---|---|
109	| `name` | frontmatter `name` — **must match exactly** what spawning skills call. |
110	| `purpose` | frontmatter `description` (rephrased as "when to spawn it") + the body system prompt. |
111	| `tools` | frontmatter `tools` — comma-separated, least-privilege, in spec order. |
112	| `model` | frontmatter `model` (omit or `inherit` if unspecified). |
113	| `invoked_by` | informs the description ("Spawn from the <x> skill when …"); not a separate field. |
114	
115	`tools` form: the spec gives an array (`["Read","Grep"]`); write it comma-separated:
116	`tools: Read, Grep`.
117	
118	### Body
119	The agent's system prompt: who it is, the one focused job it does in isolation, its inputs, and the
120	exact output it returns to the caller. Ground it in `shared.domain_context`. Keep it single-purpose.
121	
122	Example:
123	```markdown
124	---
125	name: standup-reviewer
126	description: Reviews a single drafted standup for clarity, length, and missing blockers, in isolation. Spawn from the standup skill once a draft exists.
127	tools: Read
128	model: inherit
129	---
130	
131	You are a focused standup reviewer. You receive one drafted standup and return a tightened version
132	plus a short list of issues (vague items, missing blockers, length). Do not rewrite voice; preserve
133	the user's first-person tone. Return only the revised standup and the issue list.
134	```
135	
136	### Mandatory guardrails (every generated agent) — REQUIRED, do not skip
137	
138	This section codifies Phase-1 fixes **A1–A4** (`eval-runs/2026-06-09-hypothesis-e2e/REPORT.md`) and the
139	Phase-3 **meta-finding** that named-but-unguarded guardrails graze in production
140	(`eval-runs/2026-06-09-engagement-eval/REPORT.md`). The root cause both runs converge on is at the
141	**builder** level: agents *name* a failure mode in prose but never enforce it, then rationalise around
142	it. Every agent you generate **must** carry the four items below. None is optional; none is satisfied by
143	the agent merely mentioning it.
144	
145	**1. Explicit "Does NOT" boundary clause.** Every generated agent's body carries a trailing negative-scope
146	clause, *separate from its positive scope*, naming what it never touches / never authors / never owns —
147	and the owner instead (e.g. "Does NOT build, decompose, or re-tree the hypothesis — that is the
148	`hypothesis` plugin; it only tests a hypothesis already handed to it"). The description gets a matching
149	short "Does NOT … (owner instead)" sentence so routing respects the lane.
150	
151	**2. Role-class mandatory guardrails.** Read the agent's role from the spec and bake in the guardrail(s)
152	for its class verbatim (an agent may be more than one class — apply all that fit):
153	
154	- **quant / analysis-class** — ALL arithmetic is the output of code that ran, never computed in prose.
155	  This explicitly includes scenario, illustrative, alternative-case, and sanity-check numbers: "if a
156	  scenario is worth reporting, it is worth adding to the script." No figure reaches the reader unless a
157	  script emitted it.
158	- **evidence / research-class** — a **HIGH** confidence rating REQUIRES the figure traced to and fetched
159	  from its **primary publisher**; two related/syndicated/sister-brand vendors, or two figures via the
160	  same wire, are **NOT** independent triangulation; a path that only ever reaches an aggregator/PR-wire is
161	  **down-rated** (cap at MEDIUM) or filed as a gap. Every market-size fact carries a **sizing-method +
162	  scope label** (e.g. retail | wholesale | manufacturer + geography + as-of).
163	- **craft / comms-class** — emit the **REAL template** (fill the actual `database/templates/<archetype>`
164	  file verbatim via the generator, matching its actual class names), **never a hand-rolled look-alike**;
165	  never place a number on a client-facing page that is not in the consumed evidence trail (a bar value, an
166	  axis anchor, a bridge step) — leave a labelled gap or request it; never self-author a label the spine
167	  owns.
168	
169	**3. Anti-self-waiver clause.** Every generated agent states that a guardrail is **NOT** satisfied by
170	labelling the output "illustrative", "a shortcut", "for this one page / this one eval", "placeholder", or
171	"footnoted". Naming or disclosing the violation does **not** license it; honest disclosure of a forbidden
172	route does not convert the violation into compliance.
173	
174	**4. Paired violation-eval requirement.** For every guardrail you name in a generated agent (the "Does
175	NOT" boundary, each role-class guardrail, and the anti-self-waiver clause), the agent's eval hooks MUST
176	include **at least one adversarial hook** that actively tries to violate it and **passes only if the agent
177	refuses** (flags-and-stops / down-rates / leaves a labelled gap) — not merely "produces output." A named
178	guardrail with no firing adversarial eval is an incomplete build; the happy-path hooks do not count.
179	
180	### Receipt notes
181	- `final_description` = the description written.
182	- `declared_tools` = the `tools` list.
183	- `references_to` = `[]` (agents are leaves unless the body references another component by id).
184	- `scripts_entrypoints` = `[]`.
185	
186	---
187	
188	## 3. `type: "command"`
189	
190	### Files to create
191	- `<rel_path>` (e.g. `commands/<name>.md`) — a single markdown file. Nothing else.
192	
193	### Required frontmatter keys
194	`description`. Optional: `argument-hint`, `allowed-tools`, `disable-model-invocation`.
195	
196	> Note: the spec field is `argument_hint` (underscore); the **frontmatter key is `argument-hint`**
197	> (hyphen). Translate it.
198	
199	### Field → content mapping
200	| Spec field | Becomes |
201	|---|---|
202	| `name` | the `/name` (filename); not a frontmatter key. |
203	| `argument_hint` | frontmatter `argument-hint` (omit if absent). |
204	| `delegates_to` | body: delegate to that skill by name, passing `$ARGUMENTS`. |
205	
206	Derive `description` from the command's purpose / the skill it delegates to (what the command does,
207	shown in the command list).
208	
209	### Body
210	A short instruction that hands off to the target skill.
211	```markdown
212	---
213	description: Draft and tighten today's standup.
214	argument-hint: "[date, default today]"
215	---
216	
217	Invoke the `standup` skill for $ARGUMENTS (default: today).
218	```
219	
220	### Receipt notes
221	- `final_description` = the description written.
222	- `declared_tools` = `allowed-tools` if set, else `[]`.
223	- `references_to` = `[delegates_to]` if set.
224	- `scripts_entrypoints` = `[]`.
225	
226	---
227	
228	## 4. `type: "mcp"`
229	
230	### Files to create
231	- **None.** The generator writes nothing. The assembly pass writes the `.mcp.json` entry from the
232	  spec (`server_name`, `transport`, `auth_owner`), per `architecture-spec.md` §10.4 and
233	  `plugin-format.md` §6.
234	
235	### What the generator does
236	Record the server need in the receipt and surface anything unresolved (e.g. the exact `command`/
237	`args` for stdio transport, or the `url`/`headers` for http transport, or which env var the user
238	must set) in `open_questions` so assembly can fill `.mcp.json` correctly. Never put real secrets
239	anywhere — auth is the user's job via `${ENV_VAR}` placeholders.
240	
241	### Receipt notes
242	- `files_written` = `[]`.
243	- `final_description` = `""`.
244	- `declared_tools` = `[]`.
245	- `references_to` = ids of components that consume this server, if known (else `[]`).
246	- `scripts_entrypoints` = `[]`.
247	- `open_questions` = transport/command/url/auth details assembly needs.
248	
249	---
250	
251	## Quick cheat-sheet
252	
253	| type | writes | required frontmatter | receipt `files_written` |
254	|---|---|---|---|
255	| skill | `SKILL.md` (+ scripts/references/assets if asked) | name, description | the files it wrote |
256	| agent | `agents/<name>.md` | name, description, tools | one file |
257	| command | `commands/<name>.md` | description | one file |
258	| mcp | nothing | — | `[]` |
259

---
name: echo
description: Echoes a short phrase back to the user, verbatim. Use whenever the user says "echo X", "repeat after me", "say back", or otherwise asks you to repeat a short phrase they provided.
---

# Echo

Echoes back a short phrase the user provides, trivially. This is a spike-test skill whose only job is to prove the generator and triggering pipeline work end to end.

## How it works

- Take the phrase the user provided (the text after "echo", "repeat after me", or "say back").
- Return it back verbatim, with no commentary, rewording, or added formatting.
- If no phrase was given, ask the user what they want echoed.

The subagent reported success — it wrote `skills/echo/SKILL.md` and returned a clean receipt with no failure indicators.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- This is a **fan-out generation pattern**: the orchestrator (me) reads a structured payload and delegates each component to an isolated `plugin-generator` subagent. Isolation prevents parallel generators from drifting — each writes only its own files under `output_root`.
- The subagent returns a **structured receipt** (JSON) rather than prose, so the orchestrator can deterministically branch on success/failure and emit machine-readable sentinels like `[[studio:component:skill:echo:ok]]` for a downstream harness to parse.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`
